subject: reading
title: Grade 3 Reading — EOG Practice Test 1
instructions: Read each selection. Some selections have two parts. Read each question carefully and choose the best answer. For questions that say "Select all that apply," mark every correct answer.

passages.title: The Lopsided Pot
passages.text:
Part 1

Nina sat at the spinning wheel in her grandmother's pottery shop. All her cousins could make smooth, round bowls. Their pots stood in a neat row on the shelf, shining like new moons. Nina's pots always leaned to one side.

"Mine is lopsided again," Nina groaned, pushing the wet clay away. "I will never be good at this."

Grandmother wiped her hands on her apron and came to sit beside her. "When I was your age," she said softly, "my pots leaned more than yours do now. A pot is not ruined because it leans. Sometimes a lean is the start of something new."

Nina did not believe her. She watched her cousin Theo lift a perfect, even pot from the wheel. Everyone clapped. Nina hid her crooked pot behind a sack of dry clay where no one could see it.

Part 2

The next week, a flood of rain swept through the village. Water rushed into the shop and knocked the neat row of round pots to the floor. They cracked and broke into pieces. When the family came back, the shelf was bare.

Then Grandmother reached behind the sack of dry clay. She pulled out Nina's lopsided pot, dry and whole. The lean had let the pot rest against the wall, so it had not rolled off the shelf and shattered.

"This crooked little pot is the only one that lasted," Grandmother said, holding it up to the light. The cousins gathered around to look. Theo smiled at Nina. "Show me how you made it lean like that," he said.

Nina laughed. She no longer wished her pot looked like everyone else's. For the first time, she was glad it was her own.

passages.title: The Smallest Helper
passages.text:
Part 1

In the warm grass of the meadow lived a colony of ants. Every ant had a job. The big ants carried heavy seeds. The fast ants ran messages from one tunnel to the next. But Pip was the smallest ant of all, and no job seemed to fit her.

"You are too little to carry a seed," said a big ant.

"You are too slow to run a message," said a fast ant.

Pip felt useless. She wandered to the far edge of the meadow, where the other ants rarely went. There she found a thin crack in the dry earth, and from it came the cool smell of water.

Part 2

That summer, the meadow grew dry as dust. The stream the ants drank from shrank to a trickle and then to nothing. The big ants and the fast ants searched everywhere, but they were too large to squeeze into the narrow places where water might hide.

Pip remembered the crack at the edge of the meadow. She slipped easily through the tiny gap, down into the cool dark, and there she found a hidden pool of clean water. She hurried back and led the others to the spot.

One by one, the bigger ants tried to follow her down, but only Pip was small enough to fit. So Pip carried water up, drop by drop, until the whole colony had enough to drink.

"Being small," said the oldest ant, "turned out to be exactly the right size." Pip glowed with pride. She had a job after all, and it was a job only she could do.

passages.title: The Journey of a Raindrop
passages.text:
Part 1

Have you ever wondered where rain comes from, or where it goes after it falls? Water is always moving in a great loop called the water cycle. The same water has been traveling around our planet for millions of years.

The journey begins with the sun. The sun heats the water in oceans, lakes, and rivers. When water gets warm enough, it turns into a gas called water vapor and rises into the air. This step is called evaporation. You cannot see water vapor, but it is all around you.

High in the sky, the air is cold. The cold air cools the water vapor and turns it back into tiny drops of water. Millions of these tiny drops gather together to form clouds. This step is called condensation.

Part 2

Clouds drift across the sky, pushed by the wind. Inside a cloud, the tiny water drops bump into one another and join. Slowly the drops grow heavier and heavier. When they become too heavy to float, they fall to the earth as rain, snow, or hail. This step is called precipitation.

Once the water reaches the ground, its journey is still not over. Some water soaks deep into the soil. Some flows downhill into streams and rivers, which carry it back to the ocean. Then the sun warms the water again, and the whole cycle starts over.

The water cycle never stops, and it never wastes a single drop. The rain that splashes on your window today may have fallen on a forest long ago, or even on the dinosaurs millions of years before that.

passages.title: The Lighthouse Keeper of Long Ago
passages.text:
Part 1

Long before bright electric lights and computers, ships at sea faced great danger near the shore. Hidden rocks and shallow water could crack a ship's wooden hull and sink it. To keep sailors safe, people built tall towers called lighthouses along the coast. At the top of each tower burned a strong light to warn ships away from danger.

But a lighthouse light did not shine by itself. A person called a lighthouse keeper had to care for it. The keeper lived in a small house beside the tower, often far from any town. Every evening at sunset, the keeper climbed a long, winding staircase to light the lamp. The lamp burned oil, so the keeper carried heavy buckets of oil up the stairs again and again.

Part 2

The keeper's work was never done. The glass around the light had to be cleaned each day, because smoke and salt would dim its glow. If the light grew weak, a ship might not see the warning in time.

During storms, the keeper's job became the hardest of all. Wind shook the tower and rain pounded the windows, yet the keeper stayed awake all night to keep the flame burning. Sailors out on the dark water trusted that light to guide them home. Many keepers said they could not sleep until the sun rose and the danger passed.

Today, machines run most lighthouses, and keepers are no longer needed. But long ago, the steady glow of a lighthouse was the work of one brave person climbing the stairs, night after night, so that strangers at sea would be safe.

passages.title: The Garden After Rain
passages.text:
Part 1

The storm has packed its bags and gone,
the gray clouds rolled away.
The garden lifts its dripping head
to greet the washed-bright day.

Each leaf is wearing little jewels
that sparkle, shake, and slide.
A spider mends her broken web
with patience and with pride.

Part 2

The worms come up to taste the air,
the robins hop and sing.
The puddles hold a piece of sky,
a small and shining thing.

So do not frown when raindrops fall
and hide the sun from view—
for every storm that comes to pass
leaves something fresh and new.

questions.questionText: According to the text, why does Nina hide her pot behind the sack of dry clay?
options: She wants to keep it safe from the coming flood.
She thinks it is the best pot in the whole shop.
She does not want anyone to see that it is crooked.
She is saving it as a gift for her grandmother.
questions.explanation: In Part 1 Nina hides her crooked pot "where no one could see it," because she is ashamed it is lopsided. She does not yet know a flood is coming.

questions.questionText: Which word BEST describes how Nina feels at the beginning of the story?
options: Bored
Frightened
Cheerful
Discouraged
questions.explanation: Nina groans, pushes the clay away, and says she will never be good at pottery, showing she feels discouraged.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "lopsided" in paragraph 1 of Part 1?
options: Leaning more to one side than the other
Very large and heavy
Smooth and perfectly round
Painted in bright colors
questions.explanation: The text contrasts the cousins' "smooth, round bowls" with Nina's pots, which "always leaned to one side," so lopsided means leaning to one side.

questions.questionText: What happens to the neat row of round pots during the flood?
options: They fall to the floor and break into pieces.
They are sold quickly before the rain comes.
They float safely on top of the water.
Grandmother carries them out of the shop.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says the water "knocked the neat row of round pots to the floor" and "they cracked and broke into pieces."

questions.questionText: Why does Nina's lopsided pot survive the flood when the others do not?
options: It was too heavy for the rushing water to move.
Grandmother had already moved it to a safe shelf.
It was made of stronger clay than the round pots.
Its lean let it rest against the wall so it did not roll off and shatter.
questions.explanation: The text explains, "The lean had let the pot rest against the wall, so it had not rolled off the shelf and shattered."

questions.questionText: What is the theme, or main message, of this story?
options: Hard rain will ruin everything that people make.
The fastest worker is always the most successful.
What makes you different can turn out to be a strength.
You should always copy the work of more skilled people.
questions.explanation: Nina's pot is saved because of the very lean she was ashamed of, and she ends up glad it is her own. The story shows that being different can be a strength.

questions.questionText: Which detail from the text BEST supports the idea that Nina changes how she feels about her pottery by the end of the story?
options: "Nina's pots always leaned to one side."
"Water rushed into the shop and knocked the neat row of round pots to the floor."
"For the first time, she was glad it was her own."
"She watched her cousin Theo lift a perfect, even pot from the wheel."
questions.explanation: At the start Nina is ashamed of her pot, but the line "For the first time, she was glad it was her own" shows her feelings have changed.

questions.questionText: Select all of the things that happen AFTER the flood comes into the shop.
options: Grandmother pulls Nina's pot out from behind the sack.
Nina hides her crooked pot behind the dry clay.
Theo asks Nina to show him how she made her pot lean.
Grandmother tells Nina that her own pots leaned when she was young.
questions.explanation: After the flood, Grandmother finds Nina's pot and Theo asks to learn from her. Nina hiding the pot and Grandmother's first talk both happen before the flood, in Part 1.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of "The Smallest Helper"?
options: The meadow is a dangerous place during a storm.
Pip uses her small size to help the colony in a way no one else can.
Big ants are always more useful than small ants.
Ants build the deepest tunnels in the whole meadow.
questions.explanation: The whole story leads to Pip squeezing into a narrow crack to find water, doing "a job only she could do." That is the main idea.

questions.questionText: Which word BEST describes Pip at the end of the story?
options: Useless
Frightened
Proud
Lazy
questions.explanation: The text says "Pip glowed with pride" because she finally had a job only she could do.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "trickle" in paragraph 1 of Part 2?
options: A loud, rushing flood
A thin, slow flow of water
A deep, cool pool
A cloud full of rain
questions.explanation: The stream "shrank to a trickle and then to nothing," so a trickle is a thin, slow flow that is almost gone.

questions.questionText: According to the text, why can only Pip reach the hidden pool of water?
options: She is the fastest runner in the colony.
She is the only ant who knows how to swim.
She is small enough to fit through the narrow crack.
She is stronger than all the other ants.
questions.explanation: The text says the bigger ants were "too large to squeeze into the narrow places," but Pip "slipped easily through the tiny gap."

questions.questionText: How does the author connect Part 1 and Part 2 of the story?
options: Part 2 repeats the same events as Part 1 from a new ant's view.
Part 1 describes a storm and Part 2 describes the same storm again.
Part 1 tells a true story and Part 2 explains how to find water.
The crack Pip finds in Part 1 becomes the way she saves the colony in Part 2.
questions.explanation: In Part 1 Pip discovers a crack with a cool smell of water; in Part 2 that crack leads to the hidden pool she uses to save everyone. The detail in Part 1 sets up the solution in Part 2.

questions.questionText: What does the oldest ant most likely mean by saying, "Being small turned out to be exactly the right size"?
options: All the ants should try to become smaller.
Small ants should never carry water.
Pip's small size was just what the colony needed.
The colony has grown too crowded with ants.
questions.explanation: Pip's small size let her reach the water no other ant could reach, so being small was exactly right for that job.

questions.questionText: Use both stories to answer this question. What is one way Nina in "The Lopsided Pot" and Pip in "The Smallest Helper" are alike?
options: Each one first feels that a trait makes them worse, but later that trait becomes helpful.
Each one runs away from home to live in a meadow.
Each one teaches a grandmother how to make pottery.
Each one is the largest and strongest in the group.
questions.explanation: Nina is ashamed her pot leans and Pip feels useless for being small, yet the lean saves Nina's pot and the small size lets Pip find water. The very trait each disliked turns out to help.

questions.questionText: What problem do the ants face in Part 2 of the story?
options: A flood washes away all of their tunnels.
The meadow dries up and their water disappears.
A bird eats most of the seeds they collected.
The big ants and fast ants leave the colony.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says "the meadow grew dry as dust" and the stream "shrank to a trickle and then to nothing," which is the problem the ants must solve.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of "The Journey of a Raindrop"?
options: Oceans hold more water than rivers and lakes.
Water moves in a never-ending loop called the water cycle.
Rain is the most dangerous kind of weather.
Clouds are made only on cold winter days.
questions.explanation: The article follows water through evaporation, condensation, and precipitation, all parts of "a great loop called the water cycle" that "never stops."

questions.questionText: According to the text, what does the sun do to start the water cycle?
options: It heats the water until it turns into water vapor.
It pulls rain down to the earth.
It cools the air high above the ground.
It pushes the clouds across the sky.
questions.explanation: Part 1 says "The journey begins with the sun," which "heats the water" until it "turns into a gas called water vapor."

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "evaporation" as it is used in the text?
options: Water warming up and turning into a gas that rises into the air
Water soaking down into the soil
Water falling to the earth as rain or snow
Water drops joining to form clouds in the sky
questions.explanation: The text says when water gets warm it "turns into a gas called water vapor and rises into the air. This step is called evaporation."

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas in paragraph 3 of Part 1 (about condensation) and paragraph 1 of Part 2 (about precipitation)?
options: Both paragraphs compare snow with hail.
Both paragraphs explain how the sun heats the ocean.
The first paragraph gives an opinion and the second proves it is wrong.
The tiny drops that form clouds in paragraph 3 grow heavy and fall as rain in the next paragraph.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 explains how tiny drops gather into clouds (condensation); the next paragraph picks up those same drops, which "grow heavier" until they fall as precipitation. The author links the steps in order.

questions.questionText: How does the author organize the information in this whole article?
options: By listing reasons people should save water
By describing a problem and then offering a solution
By comparing rain to snow and hail
By giving the steps of the water cycle in the order they happen
questions.explanation: The article moves through evaporation, condensation, and precipitation, then back to the ocean, presenting the steps of the cycle in order.

questions.questionText: According to the text, what happens to water after it reaches the ground?
options: It disappears and is gone from the earth for good.
Some soaks into the soil and some flows into streams that lead back to the ocean.
All of it freezes into ice and stays there forever.
It rises straight back into the clouds as rain.
questions.explanation: Part 2 says "Some water soaks deep into the soil" and "Some flows downhill into streams and rivers, which carry it back to the ocean."

questions.questionText: Which detail from the text BEST supports the idea that the same water has been used over and over for a very long time?
options: "The rain that splashes on your window today may have fallen on a forest long ago, or even on the dinosaurs."
"Clouds drift across the sky, pushed by the wind."
"You cannot see water vapor, but it is all around you."
"High in the sky, the air is cold."
questions.explanation: The line about rain that "may have fallen on the dinosaurs millions of years before" shows the same water is reused again and again over a very long time.

questions.questionText: What is the author's main purpose for writing this article?
options: To explain how water moves through the water cycle
To describe the author's favorite rainy day
To tell a made-up story about a talking raindrop
To convince readers to drink more water
questions.explanation: The article gives facts and steps to explain how the water cycle works, so its purpose is to inform.

questions.questionText: What is the main idea of "The Lighthouse Keeper of Long Ago"?
options: Ships made of wood are stronger than ships made of metal.
Storms at sea are more common than calm weather.
Long ago, a lighthouse keeper worked hard to keep the warning light burning for ships.
Lighthouses are the tallest buildings near the ocean.
questions.explanation: The article describes the keeper's daily and stormy work to keep the light burning so ships stayed safe, which is the main idea.

questions.questionText: According to the text, why did people build lighthouses along the coast?
options: To store oil for the ships that passed by
To warn ships away from hidden rocks and shallow water
To watch for storms coming over the land
To give the keepers a place to live near town
questions.explanation: Part 1 says hidden rocks and shallow water could sink a ship, so "to keep sailors safe, people built tall towers called lighthouses."

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "dim" in paragraph 1 of Part 2?
options: To turn the light a new color
To make the light less bright
To make the light larger
To clean the glass of the light
questions.explanation: The text says smoke and salt would "dim its glow" so the keeper cleaned the glass; if the light "grew weak," ships might not see it. Dim means to make less bright.

questions.questionText: According to the text, what did the keeper do every evening at sunset?
options: Carried buckets of water up to the light
Climbed the winding staircase to light the lamp
Cleaned the small house beside the tower
Sailed out to sea to guide the ships home
questions.explanation: Part 1 says "Every evening at sunset, the keeper climbed a long, winding staircase to light the lamp."

questions.questionText: How does the author connect the ideas about cleaning the glass and about working during storms in Part 2?
options: Both compare lighthouses to ordinary houses.
Both explain how machines run lighthouses today.
Both show the many hard tasks the keeper had to do to keep the light shining.
The cleaning caused the storms to happen.
questions.explanation: Cleaning the glass each day and staying awake through storms are both examples of the keeper's never-ending hard work to keep the warning light strong.

questions.questionText: Based on the text, why was the keeper's job hardest during storms?
options: The keeper had to stay awake all night so the flame would not go out.
The keeper had to sail out and rescue the sailors.
The keeper had to carry the ships back to shore.
The keeper had to build a new lighthouse before morning.
questions.explanation: During storms the wind shook the tower, yet "the keeper stayed awake all night to keep the flame burning," because sailors trusted that light.

questions.questionText: Select the TWO daily tasks the text says the lighthouse keeper had to do.
options: Sleep during the day instead of at night
Clean the glass around the light
Carry heavy buckets of oil up the stairs
Repair the wooden hulls of ships
questions.explanation: The text says the keeper carried "heavy buckets of oil up the stairs" and that "the glass around the light had to be cleaned each day." Repairing hulls and sleeping by day are not stated.

questions.questionText: Use "The Journey of a Raindrop" and "The Lighthouse Keeper of Long Ago" to answer this question. What is one way these two texts are alike?
options: Both try to convince the reader to visit the ocean.
Both are poems that use rhyme.
Both tell a make-believe story with talking characters.
Both are written to give the reader true information about a topic.
questions.explanation: Both selections are informational. One explains the water cycle and the other explains a lighthouse keeper's work; each gives the reader facts about a real topic.

questions.questionText: According to the poem, what has just happened before the poem begins?
options: A storm has passed and the rain has stopped.
A gardener has planted new seeds.
The sun has set and night has come.
Winter snow has covered the garden.
questions.explanation: The first lines say "The storm has packed its bags and gone, / the gray clouds rolled away," showing a storm has just ended.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the phrase "The storm has packed its bags and gone" in Part 1 of the poem?
options: The storm packed real suitcases for a trip.
The storm grew much stronger overnight.
The storm filled the garden with bags of rain.
The storm has ended and moved away.
questions.explanation: This is figurative language that describes the storm as if it were a traveler leaving, which means the storm has ended and moved away.

questions.questionText: In the line "Each leaf is wearing little jewels," what are the "little jewels" the poet describes?
options: Tiny green buds on the branches
Small stones scattered on the ground
Shining drops of rainwater on the leaves
Bright flowers blooming in the garden
questions.explanation: After the rain, the leaves "sparkle, shake, and slide," so the "little jewels" are shining raindrops resting on the leaves.

questions.questionText: What is the main message of the poem?
options: Rainy days should always make people sad.
Gardens grow best when no rain ever falls.
Spiders are the most important garden animals.
Something good and fresh can come even after a storm.
questions.explanation: The last stanza says not to frown at rain, "for every storm that comes to pass / leaves something fresh and new," which is the poem's message.

questions.questionText: What is the meaning of the word "mends" in the line "A spider mends her broken web"?
options: Fixes or repairs
Hides away from
Throws away
Builds for the first time
questions.explanation: The spider "mends her broken web with patience," so mends means to fix or repair what is broken.

questions.questionText: How does the poet describe the puddles in Part 2 of the poem?
options: As empty and dried up by the sun
As holding a piece of sky, a small and shining thing
As frozen over with a sheet of ice
As deep and dangerous pools of mud
questions.explanation: The poem says "The puddles hold a piece of sky, / a small and shining thing," describing how they reflect the sky.

questions.questionText: How does the speaker of the poem most likely feel about the garden after the rain?
options: Worried that the storm caused harm
Hopeful and pleased by its fresh beauty
Angry that the sun is hidden from view
Bored by the quiet, wet morning
questions.explanation: The speaker points out sparkling jewels, singing robins, and shining puddles, and says the storm "leaves something fresh and new," showing a hopeful, pleased feeling.

questions.questionText: Use "The Garden After Rain" and "The Journey of a Raindrop" to answer this question. What is one way these two selections are different?
options: One has two parts and the other has only one part.
One is fiction and the other is a poem.
Both are written as step-by-step instructions.
One is a poem that shares feelings, while the other is an article that explains facts.
questions.explanation: "The Garden After Rain" is a poem with rhythm and rhyme that shares feelings about rain, while "The Journey of a Raindrop" is an informational article that explains the facts of the water cycle. Both texts are written in two parts.
